text: I imagine there's a good bit of overlap with the RYM top ten - Radiohead, Kendrick Lamar, Pink Floyd. These crop up all the time in the music discussions I see.
label: analysis

text: Bowie was more of a chameleon, hitting on several musical trends that he never really invented but somehow always had a heavy hand in popularizing. Whereas Queen was just glam rock, with some creative song ideas within that single style.
label: analysis

text: It's the convergence of broad appeal and displays of technical ability. Bohemian Rhapsody was a huge hit more than once, and it's a multi-suite epic with cascading vocal harmonies and a ripping guitar solo.
label: analysis

text: With regard to Queen, they were at the forefront of big budget musician biopic hagiography. I think a generation of listeners now incorrectly view them as a world conquering behemoth based on Bohemian Rhapsody.
label: analysis

text: Reddit Taste often has a disdain for hip hop except Wu Tang and electronic music except Daft Punk. I'd argue both exceptions meet the above criteria, familiar plus novelty.
label: analysis

text: The first time I heard the term Reddit music taste was around 2012. The userbase was much smaller and more confined to 15-25-year-old white dudes, and r/music was a default sub so 90% of users were on there.
label: analysis

text: Even by the 90s it seems like the only Cream songs that got radioplay were Sunshine and White Room. Bands they were negatively compared with, like Zeppelin and Sabbath, only saw their reputation grow over the years.
label: analysis

text: Never For Ever was one of the first albums to feature the Fairlight CMI, which kind of gets her lumped in with a category of other early innovators.
label: analysis

text: She's very much like Bowie's 70s stuff, kooky and all that, but mostly very accessible. She writes very clear, legible melodies and has always enjoyed huge mainstream success.
label: analysis

text: In terms of songwriting, Bush is more conventional in that many of her songs have a single linear theme. Amos's songwriting is often more surrealist. In terms of vocals, Amos is much more conventional than Bush.
label: analysis

text: Kate is extremely unconventional. Abandoned the piano early on and relied heavily on the Fairlight to construct her songs.
label: analysis

text: She was a regular on Top of the Pops in the UK, which virtually everyone watched on a Thursday evening, so she had clear mainstream success.
label: analysis

text: She was discovered by David Gilmour, who plays on some of her early work.
label: analysis

text: Tori is much more heavily influenced by 60s and 70s prog rock than she is by Kate. Her idols were people like Robert Plant and Jimmy Page; listen to her early 90s covers of Whole Lotta Love.
label: analysis

text: Back in the USENET days the Kate Bush newsgroup was the third artist-specific music group created, after the Beatles and Grateful Dead. The archive still exists on the gaffaweb website.
label: analysis

text: She also has a song where she sings the first hundred digits of pi.
label: analysis

text: Her early stuff was done when she was 19-20, and very much in the high soprano range, which is off-putting to a lot of people.
label: analysis

text: Bush was very much part of the prog scene and came straight from it, compared to Genesis, Tull, Crimson and VdGG, all of which were successful and usually incredibly popular in the UK.
label: analysis

text: She peaked with The Dreaming, which is unapologetically weird, and then toned it down for Hounds of Love, which hit the sweet spot between accessibility and originality.
label: analysis

text: The Dreaming was not well received on release and was a difficult album for record companies to sell. It's her best but maybe least accessible.
label: analysis

text: Helter Skelter was released in 1968 and Black Sabbath's first album was released in 1970.
label: analysis

text: People are in fairly wide-spread agreement that Black Sabbath's debut is the first album that's metal the whole time, and the first by a band that can't be described as anything but metal.
label: analysis

text: Born to Be Wild is the first song to use the term heavy metal in its lyrics, in reference to a motorcycle.
label: analysis

text: The Pretty Things have the first true metal riff in late 68 with Old Man Going. Those other songs are more hard rock, but I can hear Sabbath's whole future in this one.
label: analysis

text: She's So Heavy is off Abbey Road and was recorded mostly in the first 8 months of 1969, so it certainly predates Sabbath, though not by much.
label: analysis

text: Sabbath's first album dropped in February of 1970.
label: analysis

text: Link Wray should get some cred for using distortion guitar on Rumble in 1958.
label: analysis

text: The defining musical element that ties the proto-metal bands together is using the tritone, the diabolus in musica, usually combined with the martial beat from Holst's Mars, Bringer of War.
label: analysis

text: Tony Iommi's guitar sound and riffs are a direct result of losing two fingertips in an industrial accident as a teen. It's an important aspect of the genre both sonically and aesthetically.
label: analysis

text: Black Sabbath was directly influenced by Gustav Holst's 1918 orchestral suite The Planets.
label: analysis

text: Paul talks about writing Helter Skelter in response to I Can See For Miles, which was released in 67, so he probably wrote it in 67-68.
label: analysis

text: There's a case for You Really Got Me by the Kinks in 1964 and I Can See For Miles by the Who in 1967, the latter being the inspiration for Helter Skelter.
label: analysis

text: Page and the Yardbirds were doing Dazed and Confused in 1967, before Zeppelin even formed, and Sabbath were obsessed with Zep when they came out.
label: analysis

text: Iron Butterfly's In-A-Gadda-Da-Vida from 1969, King Crimson's 21st Century Schizoid Man from 1969 and Pink Floyd's The Nile Song are all really important to the style coalescing into something distinct from rock.
label: analysis

text: 21st Century Schizoid Man can claim first metal song, but could just as easily claim first jazz fusion with those dueling sax and guitar solos, or first math-rock with the stop-and-go portions.
label: analysis

text: Iommi was definitely influenced by Cream and Clapton; in interviews both he and Van Halen credit Clapton's John Mayall and live Cream playing as the model for extended soloing.
label: analysis

text: Vanilla Fudge - You Keep Me Hanging On from June 67, Blue Cheer - Summertime Blues from January 68, and Iron Butterfly - In-A-Gadda-Da-Vida from June 68 are all defensible candidates for first heavy metal song.
label: analysis

text: I subscribe to the idea that heavy metal is a descriptor of both sound and subject matter; it must be dark in some way, which disqualifies most of the love-song candidates like the Kinks.
label: analysis

text: The difference is clear: King Crimson uses complex instrumentation and a jazz break, while Sabbath's self-titled song has no artistic pretense, just a heavy riff with no woodwind or keys.
label: analysis

text: Helter Skelter is too grounded in conventional 50s-60s rock, just louder and intentionally sloppy, which is why I'd argue it's closer to the first punk song than metal.
label: analysis

text: Billboard charts represent the most-marketed music, not the best music, and contemporary RYM charts are highly vulnerable to internet echo chambers and hype bubbles.
label: analysis

text: Give a good unknown song to Taylor Swift to put on her next record and even if it isn't a single it'll be praised by her fans for years, while the same song from an unknown goes into the void.
label: analysis

text: Say Something by A Great Big World sat at small shows until Christina Aguilera heard it, did a version with them, it won a Grammy, and now it's at over a billion streams.
label: analysis

text: Japanese City Pop from the 80s had basically zero footprint outside its home country before the 2010s, then got rediscovered by outsiders, so the popular-versus-obscure framing depends entirely on which metric and era you use.
label: analysis

text: Streaming services compress the audio, which reduces the sound quality.
label: analysis

label: analysis

text: Spotify had a backwards version of one track for years, so a song that begins with a backmasked segment played entirely reversed, which is one reason physical copies of hard-to-find music are nice.
label: analysis

text: The 40th Anniversary editions of Rush albums have a really good remaster plus a lot of good information and essays in the liner notes.
label: analysis

text: Kate is way more adventurous; she abandoned the piano early and built songs on the Fairlight, whereas Tori stayed closer to piano and harpsichord within alt rock boundaries.
label: analysis

text: Genres are slippery labels and retrospective at best. If we had perfect information we could point to the earliest uses of certain gear, picking techniques and tempos, but the labels don't support that level of scrutiny.
label: analysis

text: The early Kate Bush albums were fairly straight pop-rock with some New Wave stylings, but the lyrics always had prog-friendly narratives and stories.
label: analysis

text: Tori's voice and phrasing are notably similar to Kate's to the point that people have been making the comparison since Amos's rise to prominence over 30 years ago.
label: analysis

text: The Stones' use of a Bolero rhythm at the end of Paint It Black in 1966 inspired a handful of other riffs on Ravel's Bolero, which fed into the heavy classical-on-distorted-guitar sound.
label: analysis

text: It would ultimately be King Crimson's version of Mars in 1969 that directly influenced Geezer Butler to bring it to Earth, whose variation became the song Black Sabbath, which they renamed the band after.
label: analysis

text: Dissident Aggressor is the first Priest track appreciably heavier than anything before it, and that's not until 1977, so nothing on Sad Wings is as metal as most of Master of Reality.

text: In the UK Wuthering Heights topped the charts when Boney M and music from Grease was on the radio, so in that context it's a genuinely strange song.
